docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Hi, I'm Jorge.I write about data.

Data Engineer / BI Analyst focused on SQL, Power BI, Databricks, ETL workflows, and practical analytics documentation.

🗄️ SQL Portable SQL concepts, joins, CTEs, window functions, and practical query patterns. 📊 Power BI DAX logic, data modeling ideas, and reporting-oriented solutions. ⚡ Databricks PySpark workflows, transformations, ETL patterns, and engineering notes. 🛠️ Projects Selected technical write-ups and practical problem solving.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Hi, I'm Jorge Mena.

I work in data analysis and business intelligence, with experience in SQL, Power BI, Databricks, PySpark, ETL workflows, and reporting solutions.

This website is my technical knowledge base — a place where I organize practical notes, tutorials, implementation ideas, and lessons learned from real projects.

docs.title: What I work on
docs.text:
My work is mainly focused on:

  • SQL and query design
  • Power BI and DAX
  • Databricks and PySpark
  • ETL and data transformation
  • Reporting and analytics workflows
  • Data modeling and documentation

docs.title: What you will find here
docs.text:
This site is designed to be simple and useful.

You will find content such as:

  • technical notes
  • small tutorials
  • query patterns
  • BI and reporting examples
  • project documentation
  • practical problem-solving approaches

docs.title: Why I created this site
docs.text:
I wanted one place to document what I learn and build in a way that is structured, easy to revisit, and useful for others working in data.

Instead of keeping everything scattered across local files, notebooks, screenshots, and personal notes, this site helps me maintain a cleaner and more professional knowledge base.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
I like solutions that are:

  • practical
  • easy to understand
  • maintainable
  • focused on real use cases

My goal is not to make things look overly complex, but to make them clear and useful.

docs.title: Current topics
docs.text:
Right now, the main focus of this site is:

  • SQL
  • Power BI
  • Databricks
  • Data Engineering notes
  • Technical documentation

docs.title: SQL
docs.text:
12 ANSI SQL Features Every Strong SQL Engineer Should Know.

This document is written like a .sql study sheet — each section uses SQL comments for headers and descriptions, followed by a short example query.

docs.title: 1. JOINs
docs.text:
Combine rows from related tables using matching keys. Master INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN first, then understand RIGHT, FULL, and CROSS JOIN.

SELECT
    o.order_id,
    c.customer_name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c
    ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id;

docs.title: 2. Aggregations
docs.text:
Summarize data with functions like SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, and MAX.

SELECT
    department,
    SUM(salary) AS total_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

docs.title: 3. GROUP BY + HAVING
docs.text:
Use GROUP BY to aggregate and HAVING to filter aggregated results after the grouping step.

SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING SUM(amount) > 1000;

docs.title: 4. CASE expressions
docs.text:
Add conditional logic directly inside SQL for bucketing, flags, labels, and business rules.

SELECT
    order_id,
    CASE
        WHEN amount > 1000 THEN 'High'
        WHEN amount > 500  THEN 'Medium'
        ELSE 'Low'
    END AS order_category
FROM orders;

docs.title: 5. Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
docs.text:
Use CTEs to break complex logic into readable steps. They improve maintainability and make debugging easier.

WITH sales_per_customer AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        SUM(amount) AS total_sales
    FROM sales
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT *
FROM sales_per_customer
WHERE total_sales > 1000;

docs.title: 6. Window functions
docs.text:
Perform calculations across a related set of rows without collapsing the result set.

SELECT
    employee_id,
    department,
    salary,
    AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg_salary
FROM employees;

docs.title: 7. ROW_NUMBER() for deduplication
docs.text:
Use ROW_NUMBER() to keep the latest or best record per business key. This is one of the most common ETL patterns.

SELECT *
FROM (
    SELECT
        *,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY user_id
            ORDER BY login_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM logins
) t
WHERE rn = 1;

docs.title: 8. Subqueries
docs.text:
Use subqueries when you need one query to depend on the result of another query.

SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE salary > (
    SELECT AVG(salary)
    FROM employees
);

docs.title: 9. EXISTS
docs.text:
Use EXISTS to test whether related rows are present. It is often a clean and efficient way to filter large datasets.

SELECT *
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
);

docs.title: 10. UNION vs UNION ALL
docs.text:
Use UNION when you need duplicate removal. Use UNION ALL when you want all rows and better performance.

SELECT city FROM customers
UNION ALL
SELECT city FROM suppliers;

docs.title: 11. Set operations
docs.text:
Use INTERSECT and EXCEPT to compare result sets cleanly without forcing everything into joins.

-- Users who logged in AND made a purchase
SELECT user_id FROM logins
INTERSECT
SELECT user_id FROM purchases;

-- Users who logged in but did NOT make a purchase
SELECT user_id FROM logins
EXCEPT
SELECT user_id FROM purchases;

docs.title: 12. Recursive CTEs
docs.text:
Use recursive CTEs for hierarchies such as org charts, category trees, and parent-child navigation.

WITH RECURSIVE org_chart AS (
    SELECT employee_id, manager_id
    FROM employees
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL

    UNION ALL

    SELECT e.employee_id, e.manager_id
    FROM employees e
    JOIN org_chart o
        ON e.manager_id = o.employee_id
)
SELECT *
FROM org_chart;

Tip

Write ANSI SQL first, then learn each platform's extensions only when needed for performance, procedural logic, or engine-specific features.
